steps.title: Identify the passage's two-part structure
steps.content: The opening sentence establishes that Rev. Amos Barton did not hear his neighbors' conversation about him — a specific case of unawareness. The word "Indeed" then signals a pivot: the rhetorical question that follows moves from this one character to a universal observation about what any of us would feel learning how others perceive us.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
The passage moves from a specific character's unawareness to a general claim about human nature. Substitute each choice:
  • "The narrator expresses relief that a disagreement was resolved more expediently than expected and then indicates how the situation might have gone differently.": There is no resolved disagreement or relief in the passage; this misidentifies the content entirely. ✗
  • "The narrator comments on the fact that a character remains unaware of how he is viewed by others and then generalizes about the problem of learning others' opinions of one's own actions.": This matches the two movements exactly — Barton's unawareness, then the universal rhetorical question about all people. ✓
  • "The narrator summarizes an earlier event involving a specific character and then anticipates the later significance it will have for that character.": The second part is a present-tense universal generalization, not a forecast of future consequences for Barton specifically. ✗
  • "The narrator implies that a character is not well liked by his neighbors and then uses an extended comparison to demonstrate why their negative opinion of him is largely justified.": The narrator does not justify the neighbors' opinion; the rhetorical question is sympathetic to Barton, not accusatory. ✗
That matches the correct answer — B is correct.
